video_title: Operator-Led Private Equity at Ethos - Erik Brooks
gist: Erik Brooks, co-founder of Ethos Capital, details an operator-led middle-market PE model: 16 former C-suite executives embedded across sourcing, diligence and value creation, a deliberate one-deal-a-year concentration strategy on ~$6B AUM, a proprietary AI-powered operating system, and a durability-first underwriting bar where 'how do I lose money' must be a sub-1% black-swan exercise. Identity Digital (top-level domain registries) is the worked example.

themes.theme: Operator-led PE as an information-quality edge

insights.claim: Ethos's core thesis is that PE is an information business ('our input is not glass or metal, it's information'), so embedding former operators who ask better management-meeting questions yields better data, better interpretation, and ultimately better returns over time.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks (Ethos Capital co-founder)
insights.so_what: Reframes value creation as a diligence/information edge rather than financial engineering; LPs should test whether 'operator-led' GPs actually change information quality vs. just add advisors.
insights.quote: We don't make anything in our business... Our input to the process is not glass or metal. It's information. If the questions are better, then the information you're going to be getting is better.

insights.claim: An operator (Fahad/'FOD') running a management meeting produces deeper, better answers than a PE analyst because peer-to-peer operating dialogue ('two people speaking Portuguese... and I speak Spanish') removes the interrogation dynamic management feels with PE buyers.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Suggests durable edge comes from access to truer management signal; allocators can probe whether a GP's operators actually run meetings vs. observe.
insights.quote: Even in the best scenarios, my asking questions is a little bit of an interrogation... When FOD would run the management meetings, it was a conversation.

insights.claim: Not every operator can fill the operator-investor 'seat'; only those who are open, non-threatening and collaborative extract information, because a head-of-sales questioning another head-of-sales can read as antagonistic or competitive.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Personality/temperament screening of operating partners is a hidden selection criterion that determines whether the operator-led model actually works.

insights.claim: Cross-industry operator backgrounds produce non-obvious reframings that drive measurable margin: an education-sector partner recast a healthcare administrative-services company as 'a school' (recruit, train, place), generating ideas worth ~500bps of margin in the base case.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that diverse operating talent surfaces value-creation levers traditional deal teams miss; a tangible justification for the model's cost.
insights.quote: They're a school. How is this a school? ... In our modeling, in our base case, that was 500 basis points of margin.

themes.theme: Concentration strategy: one deal a year on ~$6B AUM

insights.claim: Ethos runs ~$6B AUM with a single deal team targeting roughly one deal per year and currently holds only four portfolio companies, deliberately concentrating firepower (16 operating partners) onto each holding.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: An unusually concentrated GP structure; LPs get high engagement per company but accept high single-name risk, a distinct risk/return profile vs. diversified buyout funds.
insights.quote: We have one deal team, which is a bit unusual in an organization that has almost $6 billion of capital under management. We're only looking to do about a deal a year.

insights.claim: The math of concentration is the key constraint: with five deals in a fund, a single zero is severely damaging, so the underwriting bar for downside protection must be extreme.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Explains why durability/loss-avoidance dominates their process; concentrated funds live or die on avoiding zeros, not maximizing upside.
insights.quote: The negative would be if you get it wrong, having a zero and a fund of five, that math is slightly problematic.

insights.claim: Ethos deep-dives on no more than six or seven companies a year and did zero deals in 2024, treating non-deployment as a feature; the freedom to not transact raises selectivity and decision quality.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Counter to deployment-pressure dynamics that plague many GPs; LPs should value patient capital cadence as a discipline signal.
insights.quote: In 2024, we didn't do a deal. That's just fine... The calm and the patience that that brings to the organization elevates the decision-m makes us more selective.

insights.claim: Ethos preserves the model across fund vintages by being strict on company characteristics but flexible on deal size (equity checks have ranged from ~$100M to $500M), letting it scale fund-to-fund without doubling headcount or deal size.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Shows how a concentrated GP scales AUM without diluting strategy; variable check size plus co-investing LPs is the mechanism.
insights.quote: the one place that we are flexible is in deal size. We've made equity investments of [a hundred] million. We've made equity investments of $500 million.

insights.claim: Ethos relies on co-investing LPs to fund larger deals without growing the fund proportionally, allowing it to keep doing the same strategy across fund I, II, III.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Co-invest appetite is structurally load-bearing for this model; LPs willing/able to co-invest get differentiated access and economics.
insights.quote: Our limited partners are folks that like to co-invest.

insights.claim: Ethos's first underwriting filter is durability: the answer to 'how do I lose money in this deal?' must be a black-swan exercise with probability the speaker assigns under 1%; durability precedes any upside analysis.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: A loss-avoidance-first underwriting standard; the relevant due-diligence question for LPs is the downside narrative, not the upside model.
insights.quote: My answer has to be some kind of black swanish intellectual exercise, the probability of which I would assign a single digit, but probably under 1%.

insights.claim: Ethos targets business models with 'no expiration date' and explicitly avoids structurally declining industries (the 'yellow pages' test), favoring sectors with macro tailwinds rather than ones at risk of obsolescence.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Sector selection screens out terminal-decline risk up front; a useful frame for evaluating any long-hold buyout target's durability.
insights.quote: we don't want to invest in a business that has an expiration date.

insights.claim: Ethos deliberately under-levers acquisitions because it never wants a company's operating strategy dictated by its capital structure, a lesson Brooks says he learned from a prior mistake.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Conservative leverage trades some IRR for operational flexibility and downside protection; differentiates them from max-leverage buyout shops.
insights.quote: We're conservative in how we lever these businesses up front because we never want the operating strategy of a business to be dictated by the capital structure. I've made that mistake before.

insights.claim: Brooks's formative Eastern Europe privatization experience (mid-late 1990s) taught that 7-8x returns came from circumstances, not genius, after a portfolio bought at 10 cents on the dollar rose to ~70-80 cents then collapsed to ~4 cents in the EM crisis.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Origin of the firm's humility-about-luck and loss-aversion ethos; a reminder that high-multiple EM gains can fully reverse.
insights.quote: your 70 cents on the dollar goes to 4 cents on the dollar. You recognize that it was not your genius. In fact, it was the circumstances.

themes.theme: Identity Digital / DNS as the ideal business model

insights.claim: Identity Digital, the world's largest top-level-domain owner, exemplifies Ethos's ideal: ~60s% EBITDA margins, double-digit topline growth, 98% free-cash-flow conversion, and the highest customer-renewal probability Brooks has seen by vintage cohort — 'almost a mirror image' of VeriSign.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Quantifies the financial profile Ethos seeks; VeriSign is offered as a public comp/benchmark for DNS-registry economics.
insights.quote: The business has ebidal margins in the '60s is growing topline double digit has 98% free cash flow conversion very much like VeraSign for example almost a mirror image.

insights.claim: Domain registration is mission-critical yet near-zero cost (~$30/year on average), producing structurally sticky demand — customers 'spend more on coffee, soda, and pens' than on the digital identity their business depends on.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: The mission-critical-but-trivial-cost profile is a generalizable hunting ground for pricing power and retention in any portfolio.
insights.quote: It costs on average for this company's domain names $30 a year. We spend more on coffee, soda, and pens individually than we do on our digital identities.

insights.claim: The .com registry alone has ~173 million registrations, indicating the scale of the top-level-domain market Ethos targeted within its digital-infrastructure theme.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Sizes the DNS registry opportunity; relevant context for VeriSign and registry-adjacent investments.
insights.quote: About 173 million.com registrations.

insights.claim: On Identity Digital, Ethos refinanced from a SOFR+625 unitranche (paying ~10-12%) into a securitized asset-backed loan at a fixed ~6.8%, saving roughly $50M a year, requiring three partners near-full-time to build bankruptcy-remote subsidiaries, backup-servicer agreements and rating-agency meetings.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Concrete value-creation lever: capital-structure engineering (recurring-revenue securitization) can be worth tens of millions annually for high-FCF subscription assets.
insights.quote: from a sofur plus 625 unit deal that we're paying 10 12 on into a securitized assetbacked loan that we're going to pay a fixed 6.8% and save $50 million a year.

themes.theme: Value-creation engine: acceleration vectors and the FIT filter

insights.claim: Ethos requires a target to generate 20-30 candidate value-creation ideas ('acceleration vectors') across strategy, market and operations; if a company yields only four or five real ideas it is 'not an Ethos company' because there is no alpha to add.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Idea-density is itself a deal screen — a usable test for whether an operationally-intensive GP can actually move a given asset.
insights.quote: If we only come up with four or five ideas and it's not an ethos company, there's no alpha in our participating... It's got to have a lot of ideas.

insights.claim: Ethos prioritizes ideas through a 'FIT' methodology — Feasibility, Impact, Timing — narrowing 20-30 raw ideas to a prioritized list of five to seven the firm actually pursues post-close.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: A reusable prioritization framework for operational value creation; the discipline avoids overwhelming management with low-yield initiatives.
insights.quote: our fit methodology, which is feasibility, impact, and timing.

insights.claim: Value-creation ideas are deliberately excluded from the base case, so any that succeed generate asymmetric upside on top of an underwriting that already clears on base-case math alone.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Structures returns as 'free options' — base case must work without operational improvements, with upside layered on; a conservative underwriting posture for LPs.
insights.quote: If any of them work and we hit our base case, then there's alpha in a system of hopefully asymmetric risk that we've created in the original base case.

insights.claim: Eight Ethos partners worked the Identity Digital deal pre-close and ten partners actively participate post-close — pricing, channel expansion, M&A, cost structure, customer strategy — illustrating the manpower concentration the model enables.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Quantifies the operational intensity per holding; a benchmark for what 'hands-on' actually means in this model.
insights.quote: Probably had eight of our partners on that deal. Once we own it, we have 10 of our partners actively participating.

insights.claim: Ethos found front-end underwriting accurate but badly underestimated the difficulty of getting management teams to own and execute value-creation ideas while meeting quarterly EBITDA hurdles and day jobs.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Implementation, not idea generation, is the binding constraint in operational PE; a caution for LPs underwriting value-creation-heavy theses.
insights.quote: Our process of how we work with management teams to help them execute and implement these ideas... was overinflated in my mind.

insights.claim: Ethos now staffs portfolio companies with added resources rather than expecting existing management to execute initiatives on top of their day jobs, after early efforts burned political capital on ideas that failed.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Real value creation requires funding incremental capacity at the portfolio company; an operating-cost reality LPs should factor into return models.
insights.quote: it's unrealistic to assume that someone's going to do their day job and all of these great things.

insights.claim: A hard-won discipline is killing failing initiatives fast; operator partners instinctively 'will it to work,' so Ethos had to teach that walking away from a stuck idea is not failure — the failure is the sunk-cost persistence.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive: the operator strength (relentless execution) is also a risk; disciplined stop-loss on initiatives preserves the alpha budget.
insights.quote: you have people who are operators who will will it to work... The failure is continuing to spend time on something that you're never going to get to work.

insights.claim: Real financial evidence of Ethos's value creation does not show up for two to three years, and the firm sometimes intentionally pulls EBITDA down near-term in service of longer-term value.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: LPs should expect a multi-year J-curve on operational value creation and tolerate near-term margin dips; early marks understate the thesis.
insights.quote: the real evidence of financial performance doesn't really show up for probably two or three years. Sometimes we pull Ebida dot down.

insights.claim: Ethos inverts traditional PE talent design: instead of partners being 'a mile wide and an inch deep' (good at everything), it builds 'an inch wide and a mile deep' specialists, so no partner must master sourcing, diligence and value creation alike.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: A reusable org-design principle; lets a firm recruit deep functional operators who would never fit the generalist PE-partner ladder.
insights.quote: We've done is created an organization of people that in some ways are an inch wide and a mile deep.

insights.claim: Every one of Ethos's 16 operating partners is someone Brooks or his co-founder had known for at least a decade, with backgrounds spanning Disney, The Gap, Forbes, Google, Verizon and Brinks across functions (sales, finance, go-to-market, technology, HR, design).
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Decade-long pre-existing relationships are the trust moat behind the model; hard for competitors to replicate quickly.
insights.quote: It is Disney, The Gap, Forbes, Google, Verizon, Brinks, you name it, across the spectrum.

insights.claim: Ethos built a proprietary AI-powered operating system feeding every email, Slack, calendar invite, note and file into a data lake and knowledge graph, giving 16 geographically dispersed partners real-time asynchronous visibility into every deal.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Information infrastructure is the operational backbone that makes a 16-partner async model viable; a tech-enabled differentiation in PE workflow.
insights.quote: Everything that we do at ethos feeds into our data lake then is run through our knowledge graph... Every email, Slack, calendar invite, every note, every file.

insights.claim: Ethos deliberately overbuilt the organization at inception (full back office, deep partner bench) to grow into the next 5-15 years without restructuring, treating excess capacity as a strategic moat.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Front-loading infrastructure trades early profitability for scalability and stability; relevant to evaluating GP institutionalization risk.
insights.quote: By design, we have dramatically overbuilt for ethos... We overbuilt it on purpose to be able to grow into the next 5, 10, 15 years.

insights.claim: Ethos learned it had to build a dedicated operating-support stack (a partner, Stephen Gold, leads operating executives, with principals/VPs/associates beneath) because senior operators needed far more execution support than a traditional investment team provides.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Operator-led models carry hidden infrastructure costs; the support layer is essential for the headline 16-partner engine to function.
insights.quote: turns out you do need some more support.

insights.claim: In every Ethos deal the sellers have stayed on significantly, which Brooks expects to persist because sellers who only want the highest price won't value Ethos's operational contribution — a self-selecting screen for aligned counterparties.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Winning deals on value-add rather than price implies Ethos avoids top-of-market auctions; a structural advantage if it holds, but caps the opportunity set.
insights.quote: if somebody's selling a business and they just want the highest price, they're not going to value what we bring to the party in the same way.

insights.claim: Ethos treats holding a company as an active, recurring buy decision: it exits only when the expected incremental rate of return from owning one more year falls below the base case, rejecting fixed hold periods.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: An evergreen-style hold discipline; LPs should understand exits are return-driven not calendar-driven, affecting fund liquidity timing.
insights.quote: It is an active decision to own a company. It is not a passive decision... Exiting for us is driven by what do we think the incremental rate of return is on owning the business for another year.

insights.claim: The Ethos partner-to-portfolio-counterpart relationship is a 'sacrosanct cone of silence' where only mutually agreed information flows out, deliberately engineered so management will share problems without fear of board-level repercussions.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Trust architecture is the precondition for the information edge to materialize post-close; a non-obvious governance design choice.
insights.quote: the relationship that the ethos partner has with their counterpart at the company is a sacrosanked cone of silence trust relationship.

insights.claim: Brooks frames the competitive goal, citing mentor Royce Yudkoff, as wanting to 'play soccer on a field tilted towards the goal of my opponent' — building a structural edge rather than competing on price in an increasingly competitive PE market.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Captures the strategic intent behind operator-led PE: manufacture structural advantage as entry multiples compress margins industry-wide.
insights.quote: I want to play soccer on a field tilted towards the goal of my opponent.

insights.claim: Brooks left ABRY Partners to found Ethos in 2019 specifically because PE had grown more competitive around 2015-16, with buyers paying ever-higher prices, eroding the edge of disciplined investing.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Datapoint on mid-2010s PE multiple inflation as the catalyst for differentiated-edge strategies; context for why operator-led models proliferated.
insights.quote: people were paying higher and higher prices being disciplined investors. That was uncomfortable.

insights.claim: Ethos's sector focus is macro-driven: financial services, supply chain, digital identity and infrastructure, and risk and resilience — areas with structural tailwinds where business models are accelerated rather than threatened by change.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Maps the specific verticals an operator-led PE firm believes offer durable, tailwind-backed buyout opportunities.
insights.quote: Those were financial services, supply chain, digital identity and infrastructure, risk and resilience.

insights.claim: Brooks generalizes media as 'technology-enabled content,' arguing media-like recurring-revenue characteristics (stable, predictable, recurring, high barriers) migrated into healthcare and business services as content digitized — the lens that lets a sector specialist expand its hunting ground.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: A reusable abstraction for spotting recurring-revenue business models outside their original industry; useful for sourcing durable assets.
insights.quote: let's think about media as technology enabled content.

insights.claim: Brooks flags that AI has fundamentally accelerated the pace of change across industries, raising the bar for what counts as a 'durable, no-expiration-date' business model relative to the prior decades of stable media/communications.
insights.speaker: guest, Erik Brooks
insights.so_what: Even durability-focused investors now treat AI-driven disruption velocity as a live risk to the moats they underwrite; raises diligence bar on obsolescence.
insights.quote: Giant air quotes based on where we are today given AI. The pace at which the game is changing in a lot of different areas has fundamentally changed.
